title: FAQ — Frequently Asked Questions
body:
## For Teachers — Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. Do I need teaching experience to apply?

**A.** No. While experience is preferred, many schools accept first-time teachers with a TEFL/TESOL certificate (120 hours or more). BRIDGE will match you with positions that fit your experience level.

경력이 없어도 지원할 수 있나요? 네. 경력이 있으면 유리하지만, TEFL/TESOL 자격증(120시간 이상)이 있으면 경력 없이도 지원 가능합니다.

### Q2. Which nationalities are eligible for E-2 visas?

**A.** Citizens of the following countries can qualify for an E-2 visa: USA, Canada, UK, Ireland, Australia, New Zealand, and South Africa. You must hold a bachelor's degree from an accredited university.

E-2 비자 대상 국적은 미국, 캐나다, 영국, 아일랜드, 호주, 뉴질랜드, 남아공입니다. 4년제 대학 학위가 필요합니다.

### Q3. How long does the process take?

**A.** For applicants outside Korea: typically 6-10 weeks from acceptance to arrival. For applicants already in Korea with a valid visa: as quick as 1-2 weeks.

지원부터 입국까지 해외 거주자는 보통 6~10주, 국내 거주자(유효한 비자 보유)는 1~2주가 소요됩니다.

### Q4. Is housing provided?

**A.** Most positions include furnished housing (single studio apartment) or a housing allowance. BRIDGE clearly indicates housing terms in each job listing.

대부분의 포지션에 원룸 형태의 숙소 제공 또는 주거수당이 포함됩니다.

### Q5. Can I choose which city I work in?

**A.** Yes. You can state your location preference, and BRIDGE will prioritize matching you with positions in that area. However, being flexible with location increases your options significantly.

네. 희망 지역을 말씀해 주시면 해당 지역 우선으로 매칭해 드립니다. 다만 지역에 유연할수록 선택지가 넓어집니다.

### Q6. What documents do I need?

**A.** You will need: a notarized copy of your degree, apostilled criminal background check, passport-sized photos, resume, and TEFL/TESOL certificate if applicable. BRIDGE guides you through the entire documentation process.

필요 서류: 공증된 학위 사본, 아포스티유 범죄경력조회, 여권 사진, 이력서, TEFL/TESOL 자격증. 브릿지가 서류 준비 전 과정을 안내합니다.

### Q7. What salary can I expect?

**A.** As of 2026, the average monthly salary for ESL teachers in Korea is 2.2-2.8 million KRW (before tax). Factors include location, experience, qualifications, and school type.

2026년 기준 평균 월급은 220~280만 원(세전)이며, 지역, 경력, 자격, 학교 유형에 따라 차이가 있습니다.

### Q8. Is BRIDGE free for teachers?

**A.** Yes. Our service is completely free for teachers. Fees are only charged to hiring institutions upon successful placement.

네. 강사에게는 전혀 비용이 발생하지 않습니다. 수수료는 채용 확정 시 기관에만 부과됩니다.

title: Arrival Checklist — Essential Tasks Upon Arrival
body:
## Arrival Checklist for New ESL Teachers in Korea

Welcome to South Korea! The first few weeks can be overwhelming, so here is a prioritized checklist to help you settle in smoothly.

### Day 1-3: Immediate Priorities

- [ ] **Activate your Korean phone** — Purchase a prepaid SIM at the airport (T-Mobile, KT, or SK Telecom booths are in the arrivals hall). You can upgrade to a full plan once you have your ARC.
- [ ] **Get transportation**: Purchase a **T-money card** at any convenience store (GS25, CU, 7-Eleven). Load it with 20,000-30,000 KRW to start.
- [ ] **Exchange currency** — Airport exchange rates are fair. ATMs at airports accept international cards.
- [ ] **Meet your employer** — Confirm your school address, first workday, and any training schedule.
- [ ] **Check your housing** — Document the condition of your apartment with photos and video (important for deposit return).

### Week 1: Medical & Registration

- [ ] **Medical examination** — Your employer should arrange this at a designated hospital. The exam includes blood tests, urinalysis, chest X-ray, and drug test. Cost: approximately 90,000-100,000 KRW (often covered by employer).
- [ ] **Alien Registration Card (ARC)** — Visit your local immigration office with: passport, employment contract, passport photos, medical results, and application form. Processing takes about 2-3 weeks.

### Week 2-4: Getting Established

- [ ] **Bank account** — Open a Korean bank account (KEB Hana, Shinhan, and Woori are foreigner-friendly). You will need your ARC, passport, and employment contract.
- [ ] **National Health Insurance (NHIS)** — Your employer will enroll you. Coverage begins after your ARC is issued.
- [ ] **Phone plan upgrade** — With your ARC and bank account, sign up for a monthly plan.
- [ ] **Register your address** — Your employer typically handles this, but confirm it has been done.

### Useful Apps to Download

- **Naver Map** or **Kakao Map** — essential for navigation (Google Maps has limited functionality in Korea)
- **KakaoTalk** — Korea's primary messaging app (your school will likely use it)
- **Papago** — Naver's translation app (Korean-English, excellent accuracy)
- **Coupang** — Online shopping with next-day delivery

### Emergency Numbers

- **Police**: 112
- **Fire/Ambulance**: 119
- **Immigration Contact Center**: 1345 (English available)

title: Welcome to BRIDGE — How It Works
body:
## Welcome to BRIDGE Job

Thank you for choosing **BRIDGE** as your partner in finding ESL teaching positions in South Korea. We are a recruitment platform dedicated to connecting qualified English teachers with reputable schools across the country.

### How BRIDGE Works

1. **Browse Jobs** -- Visit our Jobs page to explore current openings. You can filter by region, school type, salary range, and start date.
2. **Create Your Profile** -- Submit your basic information and upload key documents. Your data is encrypted and handled securely.
3. **Apply Directly** -- Found a position you like? Click Apply and fill out the application form. Your profile details are sent directly to the hiring school.
4. **Interview & Offer** -- Schools review applications and schedule interviews (usually via video call). If selected, you will receive a formal offer letter.
5. **Visa & Travel** -- Once you accept an offer, BRIDGE and your school will guide you through the E-2 visa process, document preparation, and travel arrangements.

### What Makes BRIDGE Different

- **No fees for teachers.** Our service is completely free for job seekers. Schools pay the placement fee.
- **Verified employers.** Every school listed on our platform has been reviewed for legitimacy and compliance with Korean labor law.
- **Ongoing support.** We do not disappear after placement. Our team is available throughout your contract for questions, concerns, or disputes.
- **Privacy first.** Sensitive personal data (passport info, background checks, medical records) is encrypted at rest using AES-256 encryption.

### Getting Started

Browse the other posts in this section for detailed guides on documents, contracts, housing, insurance, and more. If you have any questions, use the Inquiry form on our website or email us at support@bridgejob.co.kr.

We look forward to helping you start your teaching journey in Korea!

title: Apartment Guide — Understanding Korean Housing
body:
# Korean Housing for Teachers — What to Expect

## School-Provided Housing
Most schools provide a **furnished studio apartment (원룸)** near the school.

### What's Usually Included
- Bed or sleeping pad, desk, chair
- Refrigerator, washing machine, stove/gas range
- Air conditioning / heating
- Basic kitchenware (pots, rice cooker)
- Internet connection

### What You'll Need to Buy
- Bedding (blankets, pillows) — or school may provide
- Towels, hangers, cleaning supplies
- Kitchen utensils, dishes, cups
- Shower supplies

## Housing Allowance Option
Some schools offer 300,000-600,000 KRW/month instead of housing.
- **Pros**: Choose your own place, live where you want
- **Cons**: Finding housing alone is difficult; deposits are high
- **Key money (전세) system**: Large deposit (millions of won) = low/no monthly rent
- **Monthly rent (월세)**: Smaller deposit + monthly rent

## Tips for Your Apartment
- **Heating**: Most Korean apartments use ondol (heated floors) — very warm and cozy
- **Laundry**: Usually no dryer — use a drying rack or laundromat
- **Trash**: Strict separation required. Buy designated trash bags from convenience stores
- **Noise**: Korean apartments have thin walls — be considerate, especially at night
- **Mold**: Common in summer — use the bathroom fan and dehumidifier

## If There Are Issues
1. Tell your school/director first — they should handle maintenance
2. Document with photos before and after
3. Keep a record of all communications
4. Contact BRIDGE if your school isn't responsive

title: Tax Guide for Foreign Teachers in Korea
body:
# Tax Guide for ESL Teachers in Korea

## Tax Rate Options
Foreign teachers can choose between:

### Option 1: Flat Rate (단일세율)
- **19% flat tax** on gross salary (no deductions)
- Simple and straightforward
- Usually better for higher salaries or those without many deductions

### Option 2: Progressive Rate (누진세율)
- Same as Korean nationals
- 6-45% based on income brackets
- Can claim deductions (pension, insurance, medical expenses)
- Usually better for typical ESL salaries (2.0-3.0M range)

### Tax Treaty Exemption
Some countries have tax treaties with Korea:
- **USA**: First 2 years exempt from Korean income tax (if you meet requirements)
- **UK**: Similar 2-year exemption
- **Canada**: Partial exemptions available
- Check your country's specific treaty terms

## Year-End Tax Settlement (연말정산)
- Happens every January-February
- Your school's accountant handles the paperwork
- You may get a **refund** or owe additional tax
- Keep receipts for medical expenses, donations, transportation

## When You Leave Korea
- File final tax return before departure
- Apply for **National Pension refund** (lump sum payout)
- Pension refund processing: 1-3 months after departure
- Some countries have pension transfer agreements (check yours)

## Monthly Deductions from Your Salary
| Item | Your Share | School's Share |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| Income Tax | ~3-8% | — |
| National Pension | 4.5% | 4.5% |
| Health Insurance | ~3.5% | ~3.5% |
| Employment Insurance | ~0.9% | ~1.6% |

## Pro Tips
- **Ask your school** which tax option is better for you
- **Keep all receipts** — medical, education, transportation
- **National Pension refund** is a nice lump sum when you leave
- Consult the **National Tax Service** helpline: 126 (English available)

title: Visa Change — How to Switch to an E Visa
body:
## How to Switch to an E-2 Visa from Another Visa Type

If you are currently in Korea on a different visa (tourist, D-10 job-seeking, etc.) and have secured a teaching position, you may be able to switch to an E-2 visa without leaving the country.

### Eligible Visa Types for In-Country Change

You can generally apply for an in-country visa status change from:

- **B-1/B-2 (Tourist/Transit)** — limited cases; immigration may require you to leave and re-enter
- **D-10 (Job Seeking)** — the most common and straightforward conversion
- **D-2 (Student)** — after completing your studies
- **E-2 Transfer** — changing employers (see the E-2 Transfer post for details)
- **H-1 (Working Holiday)** — possible but varies by immigration office

### Required Documents

1. **Passport** and current **ARC** (if applicable)
2. **Visa Status Change Application Form** (통합신청서)
3. **Employment contract** signed by both you and the employer
4. **Employer's business registration certificate** (사업자등록증)
5. **Apostilled degree** (if not already submitted to Korean immigration)
6. **Apostilled criminal background check** (may be waived if already on file and you have maintained continuous residence)
7. **Passport photos** (3.5x4.5cm)
8. **Application fee**: 130,000 KRW

### Process

1. **Book an appointment** at your local immigration office via Hi Korea (hikorea.go.kr)
2. Gather all documents — check with your immigration office for any additional requirements
3. **Submit your application** at the immigration office
4. Processing time: **2-4 weeks** (you may receive a temporary sticker in your passport during this period)
5. If approved, your ARC will be updated or a new one issued

### Important Considerations

- **D-10 holders**: You must have registered your job-seeking activities and your D-10 must still be valid
- **Tourist visa holders**: In-country changes are becoming stricter. Some immigration offices may require you to exit Korea and apply from abroad
- **Timing**: You cannot work at the new school until the visa change is approved
- Always confirm current requirements with your local immigration office, as policies can change

title: Housing Guide — Accommodation for Teachers
body:
## Housing Guide for ESL Teachers in Korea

Understanding Korean housing is important before you arrive. Here is what to expect and how the system works.

### Employer-Provided Housing

Most hagwon (private academy) positions include **free housing** as part of the compensation package:

- Typically a **furnished studio apartment** (원룸) near the school
- Basic furnishings: bed, desk, wardrobe, washing machine, refrigerator, stove/range, air conditioner
- Utilities (electricity, gas, water, internet) are usually the teacher's responsibility
- The apartment is provided **for the duration of your contract** only

### Housing Allowance

Some employers offer a **housing allowance** instead of a provided apartment:

- Typically **300,000-500,000 KRW per month** in addition to your salary
- You are responsible for finding your own accommodation
- This option gives you more freedom but requires more effort and Korean language skills

### Understanding Korean Rental Systems

**Jeonse (전세)** — Large lump-sum deposit, no monthly rent:
- Deposit: 50-80% of the property's value
- Not practical for most foreign teachers

**Wolse (월세)** — Monthly rent with smaller deposit:
- Deposit (보증금): 3,000,000-10,000,000 KRW
- Monthly rent: 400,000-800,000 KRW (varies greatly by location)
- Most common option for foreigners renting independently

**Goshiwon / Officetel**:
- Goshiwon: Small furnished rooms, 300,000-500,000 KRW/month, minimal deposit
- Officetel: Studio apartments in commercial buildings, higher quality

### Tips for Finding Housing

- **Zigbang** and **Dabang** apps are Korea's main apartment listing platforms
- **Real estate agents** (부동산) are on nearly every block — they charge 0.3-0.5% of the transaction as commission
- Always get a **contract in writing** and have someone who reads Korean review it
- **Take photos** of everything when you move in — document any existing damage
- Check for **mold** (common in older buildings), heating system functionality, and water pressure

### Average Monthly Costs (Utilities)

| Item | Cost (KRW) |
|------|------------|
| Electricity | 30,000-80,000 |
| Gas (heating + cooking) | 20,000-100,000 (seasonal) |
| Water | 10,000-20,000 |
| Internet | 20,000-30,000 |
| **Total** | **80,000-230,000** |

title: Work Certificate — Experience Certificate Guide
body:
## How to Get a Work Experience Certificate in Korea

A **work experience certificate** (경력증명서) is an important document that verifies your employment history. You will need it for future job applications, visa transfers, and professional references.

### What Is an Experience Certificate?

An experience certificate is an official document from your employer confirming:

- Your name and personal details
- The name and address of the employer
- Your job title and responsibilities
- Dates of employment (start and end)
- Sometimes: salary information and reason for leaving

This is **different** from a personal reference letter. An experience certificate is a formal HR document, while a reference letter is a personal endorsement.

### When Do You Need It?

- **Changing jobs in Korea** — New employers and immigration may request proof of previous employment
- **Visa transfer** — Immigration offices sometimes require employment verification
- **Future employment abroad** — Demonstrates your teaching experience
- **EPIK/GEPIK applications** — Government teaching programs require documented experience
- **Salary negotiation** — Verified experience can justify higher pay

### How to Request One

1. **Ask your employer/HR department** directly — they are legally obligated to provide one under Korean Labor Standards Act (Article 39)
2. Request it **before your last day** when possible
3. Ask for both **Korean and English** versions
4. Make sure it includes the **company stamp** (직인) — without it, the document may not be recognized
5. Request **multiple originals** if possible

### What If Your Employer Refuses?

Under Korean law, employers **must** issue an experience certificate upon request. If they refuse:

1. Make the request in **writing** (email or KakaoTalk message for a record)
2. Contact the **Ministry of Employment and Labor** complaint hotline: **1350**
3. File a complaint with your local **Labor Board** (노동위원회)
4. BRIDGE can assist in mediating — contact us

### Template Information

A proper experience certificate should contain:

- Company letterhead
- Certificate title (경력증명서 / Certificate of Employment)
- Employee information (name, date of birth, nationality)
- Employment period
- Position and duties
- Date of issuance
- Company name, representative name, and **official stamp**

title: ARC Renewal — Updating ARC for New Employment
body:
## Updating Your ARC for New Employment

When you change employers or extend your stay in Korea, you need to update your **Alien Registration Card (ARC)**. Here is how the process works.

### When to Update Your ARC

- **New employer**: When transferring your E-2 visa to a new school
- **Contract extension**: When renewing your contract with the same employer
- **Address change**: When moving to a new residence
- **Passport renewal**: When you get a new passport
- **Visa extension**: When extending your period of stay

### Documents Required (for employer change)

1. **Current ARC** (original)
2. **Passport** (original)
3. **New employment contract** — signed by both parties
4. **New employer's business registration certificate** (사업자등록증)
5. **Letter of Release** from previous employer (고용변동 확인서)
6. **Application form** (통합신청서)
7. **Passport photo** (3.5x4.5cm) — 1 copy
8. **Fee**: 30,000 KRW (for ARC reissuance)

### Process

1. **Book an appointment** on Hi Korea (hikorea.go.kr) — walk-ins may face long waits
2. Visit your **local immigration office** during business hours (9:00-18:00, closed weekends)
3. Submit all documents at the designated counter
4. You will receive a **receipt** — keep this as proof of your pending application
5. Your updated ARC will be mailed to your registered address in **2-3 weeks**
6. The receipt serves as a temporary ARC during the processing period

### Important Deadlines

- **Employer change**: Report within **15 days** of starting at the new workplace
- **Address change**: Report within **15 days** of moving
- **Visa extension**: Apply **at least 4 weeks before** your current visa expires
- Late reporting may result in fines (100,000-200,000 KRW)

### Online Reporting Options

Some changes can be reported online through Hi Korea:

- **Address change**: Can often be done online without visiting immigration
- **Visa extension**: Some visa types allow online extension applications
- **Status check**: Track your application progress online with your receipt number

### Tips

- Always carry your ARC or the receipt with you — Korean law requires foreigners to carry identification
- If you lose your ARC, report it immediately to immigration and apply for a replacement (fee: 30,000 KRW)
- Keep a photocopy or photo of your ARC on your phone as backup
- Your ARC number is your key identifier for banking, phone contracts, and hospital visits in Korea

title: Application Process Step by Step
body:
## Application Process Step by Step

Applying for an ESL teaching position in Korea through BRIDGE is straightforward. Here is exactly what to expect at each stage.

### Step 1: Browse & Select

Visit the Jobs page and use the filters to narrow down positions that match your preferences. Pay attention to:

- **Location** (Seoul, Busan, Daegu, rural areas, etc.)
- **School type** (public school via EPIK/POE, hagwon, international school)
- **Start date** and contract length
- **Salary and benefits** (housing, airfare, severance)

### Step 2: Prepare Your Documents

Before applying, make sure you have digital copies of your key documents ready. See the Required Documents Checklist post for the full list. At minimum, you will need:

- Resume/CV
- Recent photo
- Copy of your degree
- Copy of your passport

### Step 3: Submit Your Application

Click the Apply button on any job listing. Fill in the application form completely. Incomplete applications are often rejected by schools without review.

### Step 4: Interview

If a school is interested, they will contact you (usually within 1-2 weeks) to schedule a video interview. Tips for success:

- Dress professionally, even for video calls
- Prepare a short self-introduction
- Have questions ready about the school, curriculum, and daily schedule
- Test your camera and microphone beforehand

### Step 5: Offer & Contract Review

If the interview goes well, the school will send a contract. **Read every clause carefully.** Refer to our Understanding Your Korean Contract post for guidance. Ask BRIDGE if anything is unclear.

### Step 6: Visa Processing

Once the contract is signed, your school will issue a Visa Issuance Number (VIN). Take this to your nearest Korean consulate to obtain your E-2 visa.

### Step 7: Arrival & Orientation

Your school or BRIDGE will arrange airport pickup and help you settle in. Some public school programs include a multi-day orientation.

### Timeline

From application to arrival, the entire process typically takes **6 to 12 weeks**. Start early to allow time for document apostilles and visa processing.

title: Required Documents Checklist
body:
## Required Documents Checklist

To teach English in South Korea on an E-2 visa, you must prepare several documents. Processing times vary, so start gathering these **as early as possible** -- ideally 8-12 weeks before your intended start date.

### Mandatory Documents

- **Valid Passport** -- Must be valid for at least 12 months beyond your intended entry date.
- **Bachelor's Degree (Original)** -- Any major is accepted. The degree must be from an accredited university in a country where English is the official language.
- **Apostilled Degree** -- Your degree must be apostilled by the appropriate authority in your home country (e.g., U.S. Department of State, UK FCDO).
- **National-Level Criminal Background Check** -- FBI check (USA), ACRO (UK), RCMP (Canada), AFP (Australia). Must be recent (within 6 months).
- **Apostilled Criminal Background Check** -- Same apostille process as the degree.
- **Sealed University Transcripts** -- Some schools and immigration offices require these.
- **Health Check** -- Completed after arrival in Korea at a designated hospital. Costs approximately 90,000 KRW.
- **Passport-Size Photos** -- Bring at least 5-6 copies (3.5cm x 4.5cm, white background).

### Recommended but Optional

- **TEFL/TESOL/CELTA Certificate** -- Not legally required for the E-2 visa, but many schools prefer or require 120+ hours. An accredited certificate can significantly improve your job prospects and salary.
- **Teaching License** -- Required for some public school positions, especially international schools.
- **Letters of Recommendation** -- From previous employers or professors. Helpful but not always required.
- **Experience Certificates** -- If you have prior teaching experience, bring signed letters from former schools.

### Important Notes

- All apostilled documents must be **originals**, not photocopies.
- Some countries process apostilles in 2-3 weeks; others can take 8+ weeks. Plan accordingly.
- Digital scans of all documents should be kept as backups.
- BRIDGE will never ask you to email original documents. Upload scanned copies through our secure portal only.

If you are unsure about the apostille process for your country, contact us and we will guide you through it.

title: Understanding Your Korean Contract
body:
## Understanding Your Korean Contract

Your employment contract is the most important document in your teaching experience. Korean labor law provides strong protections for foreign workers, but only if your contract is clear and compliant.

### Key Clauses to Review

**Contract Duration**
- Standard contracts are 12 months. Some schools offer 6-month contracts, but these are less common and may offer fewer benefits.

**Salary**
- Monthly salary should be stated in Korean Won (KRW). Typical range: 2.1M - 2.8M KRW/month for entry-level positions.
- Salary must be paid on a fixed date each month (commonly the 10th or 25th).

**Working Hours**
- Teaching hours (actual classroom time) vs. working hours (total time at school) are different. Ensure both are defined.
- Legal maximum: 40 working hours per week. Overtime must be compensated.

**Housing**
- Most contracts include **free furnished housing** (single studio apartment) or a housing allowance (typically 300,000-500,000 KRW/month).
- Clarify who pays utilities, internet, and maintenance.

**Airfare**
- Round-trip airfare is standard. Some schools only cover one-way. Confirm which.

**Severance Pay**
- After completing a full 12-month contract, you are entitled to **one month's salary** as severance. This is Korean law, not a bonus.

**Vacation & Sick Leave**
- Public schools: typically 18-20 paid vacation days plus national holidays.
- Hagwons: typically 10 days paid vacation. Confirm whether you choose the dates or the school assigns them.

### Red Flags

Watch out for these warning signs:

- No mention of severance pay
- "Salary will be discussed later"
- Penalty clauses for early termination that exceed one month's salary
- Requirements to surrender your passport or alien registration card
- No clear working hours or excessive overtime expectations

### What to Do

1. Read the entire contract before signing.
2. Ask questions -- a good employer welcomes them.
3. If anything seems wrong, send the contract to BRIDGE for a free review.
4. Keep a signed copy for your own records at all times.

title: Salary Guide for ESL Teachers in Korea
body:
## Salary Guide for ESL Teachers in Korea

Salaries for ESL teachers in South Korea depend on your qualifications, experience, school type, and location. Below is a general guide based on current market rates.

### Typical Monthly Salaries (KRW)

| School Type | Entry Level | Experienced (2+ yrs) |
|---|---|---|
| Public School (EPIK) | 2,000,000 - 2,300,000 | 2,300,000 - 2,700,000 |
| Hagwon (Private Academy) | 2,100,000 - 2,500,000 | 2,500,000 - 3,000,000 |
| International School | 2,500,000 - 3,500,000 | 3,500,000 - 5,000,000+ |
| University | 2,300,000 - 3,000,000 | 3,000,000 - 3,800,000 |

### Factors That Increase Your Salary

- **TEFL/TESOL/CELTA certification** -- 100,000 to 200,000 KRW/month bump at many schools.
- **Teaching license** from your home country.
- **Master's degree** -- especially relevant for university positions.
- **Prior Korea experience** -- returning teachers typically negotiate higher salaries.
- **Location** -- rural or less popular cities may offer higher pay to attract candidates.

### Deductions

Your gross salary will have the following deductions:

- **Income Tax**: approximately 3-5% for most teachers
- **National Pension**: 4.5% (your employer matches this; may be refundable when you leave Korea depending on your nationality)
- **National Health Insurance**: approximately 3.5% (employer pays the other half)
- **Resident Tax**: 0.3-0.5%

Net take-home is typically **88-92%** of gross salary.

### Saving Potential

With free housing provided, many teachers save **1,000,000 - 1,500,000 KRW per month** (approximately $750-$1,100 USD). Teachers in rural areas or those who are frugal can save even more.

### Payment

Salary is deposited into a Korean bank account, which you will open shortly after arrival. Most teachers also set up international wire transfers or use services like Wise (formerly TransferWise) to send money home.

For specific salary questions about a listing on BRIDGE, contact us and we can provide additional context.

title: Housing Arrangements & Key Money
body:
## Housing Arrangements & Key Money

One of the biggest benefits of teaching in Korea is that most employers provide **free furnished housing**. Here is what you need to know.

### Employer-Provided Housing

Most teaching contracts include a single-occupancy studio apartment (called a "one-room" or "weonrum" in Korean). Typically included:

- Bed, desk, chair, wardrobe
- Washing machine
- Refrigerator, stove or cooktop
- Air conditioning / heating unit
- Basic kitchen supplies (varies)

**Not typically included:** towels, bedding, toiletries, dishes, cookware. Budget 100,000-200,000 KRW for initial household supplies.

### Housing Allowance

Some schools offer a monthly housing allowance instead of an apartment (usually 300,000-500,000 KRW). If you take this option:

- You will need to find your own apartment.
- You may need to pay **key money** (see below).
- You gain more freedom in choosing location and apartment quality.

### Understanding Key Money (Jeonse and Wolse)

Korea has a unique rental system:

- **Jeonse (Key Money Deposit):** A large lump-sum deposit (often tens of millions of KRW) with no monthly rent. The landlord invests the deposit and returns it at the end of the lease. This system is less common for foreigners on short-term contracts.
- **Wolse (Monthly Rent):** A smaller deposit (typically 1-5 million KRW) plus monthly rent. This is what most ESL teachers use if finding their own housing.

If your employer provides housing, they handle the deposit. If you are finding your own place, confirm with your school whether they will cover or advance the deposit.

### Utilities

Monthly utility costs (electricity, gas, water, internet) typically total **80,000-150,000 KRW** depending on the season. Summer air conditioning and winter heating are the biggest expenses.

### Tips

- Inspect your apartment on arrival and document any existing damage with photos.
- Keep all receipts for repairs or issues.
- Confirm in your contract who pays for maintenance and repairs.
- If your apartment has problems (mold, broken appliances, pests), report them to your school immediately.

BRIDGE can help mediate any housing disputes between you and your employer.

title: Health Insurance in Korea
body:
## Health Insurance in Korea

South Korea has an excellent national health insurance system, and as a legal foreign worker, you are **required** to enroll.

### National Health Insurance (NHI)

- Managed by the **National Health Insurance Service (NHIS)**.
- Enrollment is mandatory for all E-2 visa holders employed for more than one month.
- Your employer handles the registration process.
- Coverage begins from your enrollment date.

### Cost

- The premium is approximately **7% of your salary**, split 50/50 between you and your employer.
- For a teacher earning 2,300,000 KRW/month, your share is roughly 80,000 KRW/month.
- The premium is automatically deducted from your paycheck.

### What Is Covered

- Doctor visits (general practice and specialists)
- Hospital stays
- Prescription medication
- Dental care (basic procedures)
- Mental health services
- Traditional Korean medicine (hanbang)

You typically pay a **copay of 20-40%** depending on the type of service and facility. For example:

- General clinic visit: 5,000 - 15,000 KRW out of pocket
- Specialist visit: 10,000 - 30,000 KRW out of pocket
- Prescription drugs: 2,000 - 10,000 KRW

### What Is Not Covered

- Cosmetic procedures
- Most dental work beyond basic care (crowns, orthodontics)
- Some advanced imaging (full-body MRI, etc.)
- Procedures deemed not medically necessary

### Mandatory Health Check

Within the first few weeks of arrival, you must complete a **health examination** at a designated hospital. This includes:

- Blood test (HIV, syphilis, hepatitis)
- Urinalysis (drug screening)
- Chest X-ray (tuberculosis)
- Physical exam

Cost: approximately 90,000 KRW. Some employers reimburse this; confirm with your school.

### Tips

- Carry your insurance card at all times.
- Many doctors in Seoul and major cities speak English. In smaller cities, bring a Korean-speaking friend or use a translation app.
- For emergencies, dial **119** (fire/ambulance) or go to the nearest hospital emergency room.
- Keep receipts for all medical expenses for your records and potential tax deductions.

title: Pension & Tax Information
body:
## Pension & Tax Information

Understanding your tax and pension obligations in Korea will help you plan your finances and avoid surprises.

### National Pension (NPS)

- **Contribution rate:** 9% of your gross salary, split equally (4.5% from you, 4.5% from your employer).
- Enrollment is **mandatory** for most E-2 visa holders.

**Lump-Sum Refund:** Citizens of countries **without** a social security agreement with Korea (including the USA, Canada, and most others) can claim a **lump-sum refund** of their contributions when they permanently leave Korea. You apply at the National Pension Service office before departure or within 5 years of leaving.

**Exceptions:** Citizens of countries **with** a social security totalization agreement (including the UK and Australia, among others) may have different rules. Check with the NPS or your embassy.

### Income Tax

Korea uses a progressive income tax system, but most ESL teachers fall into the lower brackets:

- **Flat rate option:** Foreign workers can elect a **19% flat tax** on gross income (no deductions) instead of the standard progressive rates. For most ESL teachers, the standard rate is lower.
- **Standard progressive rate:** The first 14 million KRW of annual income is taxed at 6%, and 14M-50M at 15%. With deductions, most teachers pay an effective rate of **3-5%**.
- **Resident tax:** An additional 10% of your income tax amount (not your salary).

### Year-End Tax Settlement

In January/February, your employer will conduct a **year-end tax settlement** ("yeonmal jeonsan"). This is similar to filing taxes. You may receive a small refund or owe a small additional amount.

### What You Need to Do

1. Obtain your **Alien Registration Card (ARC)** -- required for opening a bank account and tax purposes.
2. Your employer handles tax withholding from each paycheck.
3. Keep records of any additional deductions you may be eligible for (medical expenses, donations, etc.).
4. Before leaving Korea, visit the NPS office to apply for your pension refund.
5. Request a **Certificate of Tax Payment** from your district tax office if needed for your home country tax filing.

### Important

Some countries (like the USA) require citizens to **report worldwide income**, even if taxes are paid in Korea. Consult a tax professional in your home country about the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion or applicable tax treaties.

title: Airport Pickup & Settlement Guide
body:
## Airport Pickup & Settlement Guide

Arriving in a new country can be overwhelming. Here is a step-by-step guide for your first days in Korea.

### Before You Fly

- Confirm your flight details with your school at least one week in advance.
- Pack essentials in your carry-on: passport, contract copies, school contact info, some Korean Won (200,000-300,000 KRW recommended), and any medications you need.
- Download useful apps: **KakaoTalk** (Korea's main messenger -- your school will likely contact you here), **Naver Map** or **KakaoMap**, **Papago** (translation).

### Airport Arrival (Incheon International Airport)

1. **Immigration:** Present your passport with E-2 visa. The officer may ask about your employer and contract length.
2. **Baggage claim:** Collect your luggage.
3. **Customs:** If you have nothing to declare, use the green lane.
4. **Meeting point:** Your school representative or BRIDGE coordinator will meet you in the arrivals hall. Look for a sign with your name.

If no one is there, do not panic. Contact your school via KakaoTalk or call the emergency number provided in your welcome packet.

### First Week Checklist

- **Alien Registration Card (ARC):** Your employer should take you to the local Immigration Office within 90 days, but ideally within the first 2 weeks. Bring your passport, contract, passport photos, and the application fee (30,000 KRW). Processing takes 2-3 weeks.
- **Bank Account:** Open a Korean bank account (Hana, Shinhan, KB Kookmin, or Woori are foreigner-friendly). You need your passport and ARC (some banks allow opening with just a passport, but functionality will be limited).
- **Phone:** Get a Korean phone number. You can get a prepaid SIM at the airport or sign up for a plan once you have your ARC. Budget 40,000-60,000 KRW/month for a standard plan.
- **Health Check:** Schedule your mandatory medical exam (see Health Insurance post).
- **Explore Your Neighborhood:** Find the nearest convenience store, grocery store, subway station, and hospital.

### Transportation

- Get a **T-money card** (available at convenience stores) for buses and subway. Tap to pay; auto-recharge options available.
- Buses and subways run from approximately 5:30 AM to midnight.
- Taxis are affordable. Base fare is approximately 4,800 KRW. Use the KakaoTaxi app for easy hailing.

### Your First Days at School

- Arrive early and dress professionally.
- Bring your documents (passport, degree, ARC receipt).
- Ask about the daily schedule, classroom expectations, and who to contact for help.

title: Teaching Materials & Resources
body:
## Teaching Materials & Resources

Whether you are a first-time teacher or an experienced educator, having the right resources makes a huge difference. Here is a curated list of materials and tools for ESL teachers in Korea.

### School-Provided Materials

Most schools provide:

- **Textbooks** -- Your school will assign a curriculum and textbooks. Public schools typically use government-approved texts; hagwons use their own branded materials.
- **Classroom supplies** -- Whiteboard markers, flashcards, basic stationery.
- **Technology** -- Most classrooms have a projector or TV screen and a computer.

### Free Online Resources

- **ESL Library** (eslflow.com) -- Free printable worksheets, lesson plans, and activity ideas sorted by level.
- **British Council Teaching English** (teachingenglish.org.uk) -- Lesson plans, articles, and professional development.
- **Busy Teacher** (busyteacher.org) -- Thousands of free printable worksheets.
- **ISL Collective** (en.islcollective.com) -- User-submitted worksheets and video lessons.
- **Waygook.org** -- Korea-specific ESL teaching community with shared lesson plans and advice.

### Recommended Apps for the Classroom

- **Kahoot!** -- Interactive quiz games that students love.
- **Quizlet** -- Flashcard sets for vocabulary building.
- **ClassDojo** -- Behavior management and parent communication (useful for hagwons).
- **Timer apps** -- Essential for activity-based lessons.

### Activity Ideas by Age Group

**Elementary (Ages 7-12)**
- TPR (Total Physical Response) games
- Flashcard relay races
- Board games adapted for vocabulary
- Songs and chants

**Middle/High School (Ages 13-18)**
- Debate activities (simplified for lower levels)
- Role-plays and situational dialogues
- News article discussions
- Presentation projects

**Adults**
- Free-talking sessions with structured topics
- Business English scenarios
- TED Talk listening and discussion
- Writing workshops

### Tips for Success

- Always over-prepare. Have backup activities ready in case a lesson ends early.
- Use visual aids extensively -- many students are visual learners.
- Adapt materials to your students' level. A textbook activity that is too easy or too hard will lose their attention.
- Build a personal resource library from day one. Save every worksheet and lesson plan you create.

title: Public vs Private Schools Comparison
body:
## Public vs Private Schools Comparison

Choosing between public and private school teaching is one of the biggest decisions you will make. Here is an honest comparison to help you decide.

### Public Schools

Public school positions are typically arranged through programs like **EPIK** (English Program in Korea) or Provincial Offices of Education (POE) direct hires.

**Pros:**
- More vacation days (18-20+ paid days plus national holidays and school breaks)
- Consistent working hours (typically 8:30 AM - 4:30 PM)
- Co-teacher support (a Korean English teacher assists in every class)
- Government-backed contract with strong labor protections
- Professional development workshops and orientation
- Renewal bonuses (typically 1,300,000 KRW)

**Cons:**
- Slightly lower base salary (2.0M - 2.7M KRW/month)
- Less control over your schedule and curriculum
- Placement can be rural with limited say in location
- Bureaucratic processes for everything
- Desk warming during school breaks (sitting at your desk with no classes)

### Private Schools (Hagwons)

Hagwons are private academies that operate after regular school hours.

**Pros:**
- Higher starting salaries possible (2.1M - 3.0M+ KRW/month)
- More location flexibility (abundant positions in Seoul and major cities)
- Smaller class sizes (often 5-15 students)
- More creative freedom in lesson planning at many hagwons
- Faster hiring process

**Cons:**
- Fewer vacation days (typically 10 days)
- Later working hours (usually 1:00 PM - 9:00 PM or 2:00 PM - 10:00 PM)
- Quality varies dramatically between hagwons
- Higher risk of contract disputes (though legal protections still apply)
- Some require Saturday work
- Teaching younger children in the morning and older students at night (split shifts at some schools)

### Which Is Right for You?

Choose **public school** if you value work-life balance, vacation time, and structured support.

Choose **hagwon** if you want higher pay, a city location, and more independence in the classroom.

### How to Research

- Search school names on forums like Reddit r/teachinginkorea and Waygook.org.
- Ask BRIDGE for employer reviews -- we track feedback from placed teachers.
- Ask the school for references from current or former foreign teachers.

title: EPIK vs Hagwon Teaching
body:
## EPIK vs Hagwon Teaching

EPIK (English Program in Korea) is the government program that places native English speakers in Korean public schools. This post compares the EPIK experience directly to the hagwon experience.

### EPIK Overview

**What is EPIK?**
EPIK is run by the Korean Ministry of Education. It recruits teachers from English-speaking countries and places them in public elementary, middle, and high schools across Korea.

**EPIK Requirements:**
- Bachelor's degree from an accredited university
- Citizen of a qualifying English-speaking country (USA, Canada, UK, Ireland, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa)
- TEFL/TESOL/CELTA certificate (required -- 100+ hours recommended)
- No criminal record
- Good physical and mental health

**EPIK Salary Scale (2025-2026):**
- Level 1 (no experience, no TEFL): Not available -- TEFL is now required
- Level 2 (TEFL, no teaching experience): 2,100,000 KRW/month
- Level 3 (TEFL + experience/education degree): 2,300,000 - 2,500,000 KRW/month
- Level 4 (significant experience + qualifications): 2,500,000 - 2,700,000 KRW/month

**EPIK Timeline:**
- Spring intake (March start): Apply by September-November of the prior year
- Fall intake (August/September start): Apply by March-May
- Processing takes 3-5 months

### Hagwon Overview

Hagwons hire year-round and have a much faster placement timeline (often 2-6 weeks from interview to start).

**Hagwon Flexibility:**
- TEFL is preferred but not always required
- Positions available almost every month
- Negotiate salary, location, and hours directly

### Side-by-Side Comparison

| Factor | EPIK | Hagwon |
|---|---|---|
| Application timeline | 3-5 months | 2-6 weeks |
| Location choice | Limited (can request but no guarantee) | You choose |
| Class size | 20-35 students | 5-15 students |
| Co-teacher | Yes (always) | Rare |
| Orientation | 1-2 week paid orientation | Usually none |
| Working hours | Daytime (8:30-4:30) | Afternoon/evening |
| Vacation | 18-20 days + school breaks | ~10 days |

### Recommendation

If this is your **first time in Korea**, EPIK provides a structured, supportive entry point. If you want **more control** over your placement, schedule, and salary, go with a hagwon -- but do thorough research on the specific school.

BRIDGE lists both EPIK-equivalent POE positions and vetted hagwon positions.

title: Cultural Tips for New Teachers
body:
## Cultural Tips for New Teachers

Korea has a rich culture with social norms that may differ from what you are used to. Understanding these will help you build better relationships with colleagues, students, and neighbors.

### Workplace Culture

- **Hierarchy matters.** Korean workplaces are hierarchical. Use titles (Principal, Vice Principal, Head Teacher) rather than first names. Your co-teachers may tell you to use their English name, which is fine.
- **Punctuality is essential.** Arrive at school on time or early. Being late -- even by a few minutes -- is considered disrespectful.
- **Group meals.** Staff dinners ("hoesik") are common. Attendance is not strictly mandatory for foreign teachers, but going occasionally builds goodwill. Expect Korean BBQ, soju, and lots of food.
- **Gift giving.** Small gifts from your home country are appreciated when you first arrive. Coffee, chocolate, or local snacks are safe choices.

### In the Classroom

- **Age and respect.** Students may seem quiet or reluctant to speak up. This is often cultural, not disinterest. Be patient and use pair/group activities to lower anxiety.
- **Physical contact.** Korean teachers sometimes pat students on the head or hold younger students' hands. As a foreign teacher, it is best to keep physical contact minimal and professional.
- **Korean names.** Learn your students' names and pronounce them correctly. This shows respect and builds rapport.
- **Saving face.** Avoid publicly correcting or embarrassing students. Pull them aside for private feedback when possible.

### Daily Life

- **Shoes off indoors.** Always remove shoes when entering homes, some restaurants, and certain school areas.
- **Bowing.** A slight bow is a standard greeting. Deeper bows show more respect (to elders, school principals, etc.).
- **Pouring drinks.** When drinking with colleagues, pour for others (especially elders) using two hands or with one hand supporting the other arm. Do not pour your own drink.
- **Trash separation.** Korea takes recycling seriously. You must separate general waste, food waste, recyclables, and paper. Your apartment building will have designated bins.

### Useful Korean Phrases

- Hello: Annyeonghaseyo (formal)
- Thank you: Gamsahamnida
- Sorry/Excuse me: Joesonghamnida
- Yes: Ne / No: Aniyo
- I do not understand: Jal moreugesseumnida
- How much is this?: Eolmayeyo?

### Building Community

Join local expat groups on Facebook or KakaoTalk. Attend language exchange meetups. Explore your neighborhood. Korea is incredibly welcoming once you make the effort to engage.

title: End of Contract Procedures
body:
## End of Contract Procedures

Whether you are finishing your contract, renewing, or leaving Korea, there are important steps to complete. Start this process **at least 4-6 weeks before your contract end date**.

### If You Are Leaving Korea

**Step 1: Notify Your Employer**
- Confirm your departure date in writing. Most contracts require 30-60 days notice if you are not renewing.

**Step 2: Pension Refund (NPS)**
- Visit your local National Pension Service office.
- Bring your passport, ARC, and Korean bank account details.
- Apply for a lump-sum refund. Processing takes approximately 2-4 weeks after departure. The refund is sent to your Korean bank account (you can then wire it home) or to a foreign account.
- **Important:** You must apply before leaving or within 5 years of departure.

**Step 3: Tax Settlement**
- Your employer handles the final year-end tax settlement.
- Request a **Certificate of Tax Payment** from the district tax office for your home country tax records.

**Step 4: Close Bank Accounts**
- Transfer remaining funds to your home bank.
- Close the Korean account in person at the branch where you opened it.

**Step 5: Cancel Your Phone Contract**
- Visit your carrier's store to cancel. Check for any early termination fees.

**Step 6: Return Your Apartment**
- Clean the apartment thoroughly.
- Return all keys and any school-provided items (router, laundry card, etc.).
- Document the apartment condition with photos.
- Your key money deposit (if any was in your name) should be returned.

**Step 7: Exit the Country**
- Your ARC will be collected at the airport immigration counter when you depart. This is normal.
- If you have a re-entry permit, you may keep your ARC.

### Severance Pay

If you completed a full 12-month contract, you are entitled to **one month's salary as severance**. This should be paid on your final payday or shortly after. If your employer refuses, file a complaint with the **Labor Board** (you can do this even after leaving Korea).

### If You Are Renewing

- Discuss renewal terms at least 2 months before your contract ends.
- You may negotiate a salary increase (common for renewals).
- Renewal bonuses vary: EPIK typically offers 1,300,000 KRW; hagwons vary.
- Your ARC will need to be renewed at Immigration.

### Letter of Release

If you want to transfer to a new employer, you need a **Letter of Release** from your current school. Without it, changing jobs on an E-2 visa is extremely difficult.

title: Emergency Contacts & Resources
body:
## Emergency Contacts & Resources

Keep this information saved on your phone. In an emergency, knowing who to call can make all the difference.

### Emergency Numbers

| Service | Number |
|---|---|
| Police | **112** |
| Fire / Ambulance | **119** |
| Emergency Medical Info (English) | **1339** |
| Foreigner Help Line (English, 24/7) | **1345** |
| Mental Health Crisis Line | **1577-0199** |
| Domestic Violence Hotline | **1366** |

### Key Government Resources

**Immigration Office (Hi Korea)**
- Website: www.hikorea.go.kr
- Phone: 1345 (multilingual support)
- For visa issues, ARC problems, and immigration questions.

**Korean Labor Board**
- Phone: 1350
- For unpaid wages, contract violations, wrongful termination.
- You can file complaints even after leaving Korea.
- Free consultation available in English.

**National Health Insurance Service (NHIS)**
- Phone: 1577-1000
- Website: www.nhis.or.kr
- For insurance enrollment, claims, and coverage questions.

**National Pension Service (NPS)**
- Phone: 1355
- Website: www.nps.or.kr
- For pension contributions, refund applications.

### Embassies & Consulates

Locate your embassy in Seoul:

- **U.S. Embassy:** 02-397-4114
- **Canadian Embassy:** 02-3783-6000
- **British Embassy:** 02-3210-5500
- **Australian Embassy:** 02-2003-0100
- **Irish Embassy:** 02-721-7200
- **New Zealand Embassy:** 02-3701-7700
- **South African Embassy:** 02-2077-5900

### Medical Emergencies

For English-speaking medical assistance:

- **International SOS Korea:** 02-3140-1700
- **Severance Hospital (Yonsei):** 02-2228-5800 (International Health Care Center)
- **Samsung Medical Center:** 02-3410-0200 (International Clinic)
- **Asan Medical Center:** 02-3010-5001 (International Clinic)

Most major hospitals in Seoul and Busan have international clinics with English-speaking staff.

### BRIDGE Support

- **Email:** support@bridgejob.co.kr
- **Website Inquiry Form:** bridgejob.co.kr/inquiry
- **Response time:** Within 24 hours on business days.

If you are experiencing a workplace dispute, safety concern, or any issue with your employer, contact BRIDGE immediately. We can:

- Mediate between you and your employer
- Connect you with English-speaking legal resources
- Advise you on your rights under Korean labor law
- Help you find a new position if necessary

### Advice

- Save all emergency numbers in your phone before you need them.
- Keep a photocopy of your passport and ARC separate from the originals.
- Know the address of your apartment in Korean (for taxis and emergency services).
- Inform a trusted friend or colleague of your whereabouts if you are traveling alone.
